text: The laparoscopic view reveals the upper abdomen with a large hernia defect. The peritoneum appears very thin, and there is minimal preperitoneal fat visible.

The laparoscopic view reveals the upper abdomen with a large defect and thin peritoneum, with minimal preperitoneal fat visible. The surgeon places robotic ports and uses a force bipolar instrument in the left hand.
The surgeon places robotic ports and uses a force bipolar instrument in the left hand, preparing for a potential TASDR procedure due to the difficulty anticipated with the tap approach.

The surgeon makes a horizontal incision in the midline of the peritoneum to develop the preperitoneal space, carefully bringing down the falciform ligament. As the dissection progresses laterally, the peritoneum appears extremely thin with minimal preperitoneal fat.

The surgeon makes bilateral incisions through the posterior rectus sheath, followed by vertical incisions just lateral to the linea alba. These incisions connect the retro-rectus space with the pre-peritoneal space.

The surgeon begins dissecting the right retro-rectus space, aiming to reach the costal margin. The dissection continues in both the retro-rectus and pre-peritoneal spaces, with careful attention to avoid cutting the peritoneum or damaging the linea alba before dividing the posterior rectus sheath.

The surgeon begins dissecting the right retro-rectus space, progressing towards the costal margin. The dissection continues in both the retro-rectus and pre-peritoneal spaces, taking care to avoid cutting the peritoneum or damaging the linea alba. The caudal edge of the hernia sac is visible.

The surgeon identifies the caudal edge of the hernia sac and carefully dissects it, staying close to the fascia to preserve the sac tissue. The patient's skin over the sac appears extremely thin and purplish, indicating a translucent dermis.

The surgeon carefully dissects the hernia sac, staying close to the fascia to preserve sac tissue integrity. The patient's skin over the sac appears purplish and translucent, indicating thin dermis. The surgeon identifies and follows a distinct areolar plane, facilitating the removal of the hernia sac without cauterizing near the dermis.
The surgeon identifies and dissects along a clear areolar plane outside the hernia sac, facilitating the removal of the sac without the need for cauterization near the thin dermis.

The surgeon is at the cephalad edge of the hernia sac, carefully maintaining its attachments to the peritoneum under the xiphoid and diaphragm. The surgeon searches for the left lateral edge of the sac, ensuring precision to avoid injury.

The surgeon is at the cephalad edge of the hernia sac, attempting to maintain its attachments to the peritoneum beneath the xiphoid process and diaphragm. The left lateral edge of the sac is identified, but it is revealed to be connective tissue, which is then divided.

The surgeon identifies connective tissue initially mistaken for the hernia sac and divides it using cautery. The surgical field is clear of bowel or critical structures, allowing for generous use of cautery to maintain hemostasis.
The surgeon uses cautery generously to maintain hemostasis while working in the upper region of the surgical field, where bowel and other critical structures are not present.

The surgeon completes the division of the linea alba up to the level of the xiphoid, transitioning the hernia sac from the anterior abdominal wall into the peritoneum beneath the xiphoid and diaphragm. Dissection begins underneath the diaphragm and xiphoid, navigating the complex area where the diaphragm may be pulled down, potentially enlarging the hernia.

The surgeon completes the division of the linea alba up to the level of the xiphoid, preparing to dissect underneath the diaphragm and xiphoid. The hernia sac transitions from the anterior abdominal wall into the peritoneum in this region.

The surgeon prepares to perform an upper tar to transition from the retroactive space into the subdiaphragmatic space. The focus is on identifying diaphragm fibers to maintain proximity and avoid detaching the diaphragm from the costal margin, which could exacerbate the hernia.

The surgeon prepares to transition from the retroactive space into the subdiaphragmatic space by performing an upper TAR. The right hemidiaphragm fibers are visible, and the surgeon carefully dissects along these fibers to maintain the correct plane. The muscle meets the central tendon, and the surgeon avoids cutting through the central tendon to prevent entering the pericardium. A fatty plane is identified, and dissection continues along the diaphragm. The surgeon cuts the diaphragm contribution to the posterior rectus sheath, preparing to transition into the space behind the transverse abdominus.

The surgeon identifies the right hemidiaphragm fibers and carefully dissects along them, maintaining the correct plane to avoid disorientation. The muscle meets the central tendon, and the surgeon adjusts to stay on the peritoneal side, avoiding the central tendon and potential entry into the pericardium. A fatty plane is found, and dissection continues along the diaphragm.

The surgeon cuts the diaphragm's contribution to the posterior rectus sheath, inadvertently creating a small hole in the peritoneum. They cauterize small vessels in the diaphragm to prevent bleeding, then transition from the subdiaphragmatic space to the space between the transverse salus fascia and the transverse abdominus, dividing connective tissue between these spaces.

The surgeon accidentally creates a small hole in the peritoneum while working in the subdiaphragmatic space. They ensure the small vessels in the diaphragm are cauterized to prevent bleeding. The surgeon transitions from the subdiaphragmatic space to the space between the transversalis fascia and the transverse abdominus, dividing the connective tissue between these spaces. The diaphragm and transverse abdominus muscle are exposed, with the transversalis fascia separated from them.

The surgeon has connected the subdiaphragmatic space with the space between the transverse abdominus muscle and the transverse salus fascia. Both the diaphragm and transverse abdominus muscle are exposed, with the transverse salus fascia pulled down. This marks the lateral extent of the TAR procedure, facilitating a wide mesh overlap under the diaphragm.

The surgeon reaches the lateral extent of the TAR procedure, dissecting under the diaphragm on the patient's left side. The dissection is performed close to the muscle to avoid entering the Morgagni space. Around the central tendon, the surgeon carefully separates the peritoneum, proceeding millimeter by millimeter due to its sticky nature.

The surgeon dissects the patient's left side, carefully maneuvering under the diaphragm and staying close to the muscle to avoid entering the Morgagni space. Around the central tendon, the peritoneum is carefully separated, progressing millimeter by millimeter to prevent cutting through the tendon.

The surgeon navigates the dissection by staying on the peritoneal side, successfully separating the preperitoneal fat from the central tendon. The central tendon is exposed smoothly, facilitating the planned dissection for optimal mesh overlap in the cephalad direction.

The surgeon navigates into an unfamiliar space, initially suspecting it to be intraperitoneal, but the preperitoneal fat separates easily from the central tendon, facilitating the dissection. The central tendon is quickly exposed, allowing for planned mesh overlap in the cephalad direction. The beating heart is visible through the central tendon.

The surgeon observes the heart beating through the central tendon while performing an upper abdominal TAR. The incision is made in the posterior lumen of the internal oblique and transverse abdominis to transect the upper posterior rectus sheath. Two sternotomy wires are visible, surrounded by scar tissue. The surgeon uses the diaphragm muscle as a guide, staying deep to it to maintain the correct plane.

The surgeon stays underneath the diaphragm and begins incising the posterior lumen of the internal oblique and transverse abdominis to transect the upper posterior rectus sheath. Two sternotomy wires are visible, surrounded by scar tissue. The surgeon carefully divides the posterior lumbar and anterior oblique, then scoops the transverse abdominus muscle to complete its division. The white layer, identified as the diaphragm contribution to the posterior rectus sheath, is cut, allowing entry between the transverse abdominus muscle and the transverse salus fascia.

The surgeon divides the posterior lumbar and anterior oblique muscles, then scoops the transverse abdominus muscle to complete its division. The white layer, identified as the diaphragm contribution to the posterior rectus sheath, is cut. The surgeon navigates between the transverse abdominus muscle and the transverse salus fascia, dividing the intervening fascial tissue to combine the space under the diaphragm with the space under the transverse abdominus. Adequate space is created for the upper lateral part of the mesh, achieving good cephalad dissection and overlap, with sufficient lateral space on both sides.

The surgeon divides the intervening fascial tissue to combine the space under the diaphragm with the space under the transverse abdominis. As the dissection progresses laterally, the tissue becomes mostly areolar, facilitating the creation of adequate space for the upper lateral part of the mesh. The cephalad dissection is extensive, providing good overlap, and the retrolex space is sufficiently wide, negating the need for additional lateral dissection.
The surgeon closes the hernia defect vertically using a continuous suture technique, as the defect is wider than it is tall.

The surgeon uses an OV-Lock suture to close a wide subxiphoid defect vertically. The suture is initially laid out without tightening to manage the length and facilitate closure. The surgeon progressively tensions the suture, allowing the muscle to elongate and the abdominal wall to relax, aiding in the closure of the defect.

The surgeon lays out an eighteen-inch suture without tightening it initially, progressively tensioning it to elongate the muscle and relax the abdominal wall, facilitating the closure of a subxiphoid defect near the costal margin and sternum.

The surgeon carefully pulls the suture snug without forcing it, allowing the muscle to relax during closure. The subcutaneous tissue is occasionally included in the suture bites due to the patient's thin skin. Pneumoperitoneum is reduced to eight millimeters mercury to facilitate closure. The surgeon continues to place stitches, later returning to tighten them further, ensuring the defect closes without excessive force that could tear the tissue.

The surgeon carefully places sutures in the subcutaneous tissue, avoiding excessive tension due to the patient's thin skin. The pneumoperitoneum is reduced to eight millimeters of mercury to facilitate closure. The surgeon gently pulls the sutures snug without forcing them, allowing the muscle to relax. As the closure nears completion, the sutures are progressively tightened, bringing the tissue together without breaking or fraying. Two sternotomy wires are visible, which will be removed later.

The surgeon identifies two sternotomy wires that will be removed later. They complete the final stitch on the patient's left side, ensuring that each suture bite includes both the anterior and posterior fascia layers to prevent suture pull-through. The defect visibly comes together using the progressive tensioning method.

The surgeon completes the final stitch on the patient's left side, ensuring that each suture bite captures both the anterior and posterior fascia layers. The defect visibly comes together smoothly using the progressive tensioning method.

The surgeon cinches the suture tightly, pulling it posteriorly or towards the feet to avoid sawing through the tissue. The force bipolar is used to brace the tissue as the suture is pulled downward, ensuring the fascia is not ripped.
The surgeon cinches the suture tightly by pulling it posteriorly or towards the feet, using a force bipolar to brace the tissue and prevent tearing through the fascia.

The surgeon runs the suture back to reinforce the closure, then measures the mesh for placement. The mesh provides at least seven centimeters of superior overlap over the defect, with the space measuring approximately sixteen centimeters. A fifteen by fifteen centimeter piece of Bard soft mesh is prepared for use, ensuring adequate inferior overlap.

The surgeon runs the suture back to reinforce the closure, then measures the mesh, ensuring a superior overlap of at least seven centimeters above the defect. The space measures approximately sixteen centimeters, and the surgeon plans to use a fifteen by fifteen centimeter piece of Bard soft mesh, confirming good inferior overlap of at least seven centimeters.
The surgeon completes running the suture back along the defect, taking small bites of tissue. The mesh is measured and prepared for insertion once the suturing is completed.
The surgeon completes running the suture back along the incision line, taking small bites of tissue. The surgical assistant prepares the mesh for insertion by cutting it to the measured size.

The surgeon locks the suture to prevent unraveling under tension, completing the last stitch. A large space is visible, created for the placement of medium weight macroporous polypropylene mesh. The mesh is positioned with excellent overlap, requiring minimal securing. The surgeon places a few 3-0 Vicryl sutures in the center, guided by a blue or purple line drawn for orientation.

The surgeon locks the suture to prevent unraveling under tension, completing the last bite. A large space is visible, filled with medium weight macroporous polypropylene mesh. The mesh is secured with a few 3-0 Vicryl sutures in the center, guided by a blue or purple line drawn for orientation.
